See this comment in main_test.py:
```
 def test_main(self):
        """Main entry point."""
        # All this code must run inside one single test, otherwise strange
        # things happen.
```

When working on main_test.py I noticed that you can't initialize CEF in one test run some other tests and then shutdown CEF in last test. I couldn't make it work with python's unittest module, strange things happened. The solution that worked was to run all tests inside on single test (test_main method) and introduce "subtest_message" function to report about results of various tests.

You can however run to separate tests by using IsolatedTest feature. See isolated_test.py - there are two classes defined IsolatedTest1 and IsolatedTest2 which run using separate python processes. This is the only way for CEF to work, because CEF can be initialized/shutdown only once during app lifetime.

I think the easiest thing is to define test_osr() and put both test_dragdrop() and test_dragdata() inside there. I don't mean to put all the code from test_dragdata() inside test_osr(), just make a call from there and the code should be in some separate function as it is now, but just don't make it a separate test. 

The test_dragdata code seems to work now, however this seems to be invalid. Most of CEF API is intended to be called only after CEF was initialized. In your case CEF is initialized in test_dragdrop(), however this is a separate test and it shuts down CEF at the end, so after shut down you are not allowed to call any of CEF API anymore. You shouldn't instantiate cef.DragData() aftert CEF was shut down.

Ah, okay, that makes perfect sense then. From <https://docs.python.org/2/library/ctypes.html#ctypes.byref>:

> Returns a light-weight pointer to obj, which must be an instance of a ctypes type.

Meaning that in Python, `ctypes.byref(thing)` is (roughly) equivalent to `&thing` in C. It's used here in places like:

```py
... _ssl.d2i_ECPrivateKey(ctypes.byref(self.k), ...)
```

Because `self.k` represents a `EC_KEY*` and the C [signature for d2i_ECPrivateKey](https://www.openssl.org/docs/manmaster/crypto/d2i_ECPrivate_key.html) is:

```c
... d2i_ECPrivateKey(EC_KEY **key, ...)
```

Hopefully that all makes sense.

In other words, now that you pointed out (and I bothered to read) the `ctypes` docs, the "cast" looks entirely appropriate. It was my mistake to think that it wasn't. I'll clean up the code (and my comment).

I think what might be the problem is the name of the `page_size` parameter.  Its purpose is to dictate the batch size for Result iterations.  Perhaps a better name would be `batch_size`.  We use skip and limit in the iterator logic along with the page_size to manage the stream of documents.  This is the reason why we don't allow skip and limit parameters on iterations of Results/QueryResults.  So in this example the total number of documents matching the query is 5 but the page_size is set to 3 which tells the iterator to retrieve batches of at most 3 documents at a time until you've retrieved all of the documents matching the query.

    self.connection.logger.exception('getaddrinfo failed')

I'm not familiar enough with SETA to know if this is 100% correct, but my understanding is that we're trying to solve this problem:
* There are two tasks that get run, analyze_failures and load_preseed
* analyze_failures does different things depending on whether some table is empty or not
* analyze_failures needs to run at least once where it sets things as low priority (which it only does if the table is empty)
* load_preseed adds things to this table, making it non-empty
* therefore if load_preseed runs before analyze_failures does for the first time, then analyze_failures never does its "first run" type behaviour

It seems that there are 4 ways to fix this:
1) make load_preseed check to see if analyze_failures has ever been run, otherwise it just does nothing (thereby it can be safely be run at any point, and will bail with a warning if too soon)
2) make analyze_failures use a better way of determining whether to do the "first run" behaviour than checking if some table is empty
3) force analyze_failures to always run before load_preseed by always doing both at the same time
4) make analyze_failures delete everything that table before running itself and then load_preseed

Option 3 is what added minutes to the Vagrant provision and deploy times (bug 1329685).
Option 4 is the current PR's approach.

...which leaves option 1/2, or any others I've missed?

I think I must be misunderstanding something about your comment or the code, so let me over-explain what I was trying to say so we can get on the same page.

Consider this code, using `HashClient`:

```
client = HashClient([("server1", 11211), ("server2", 11211), ("server3", 11211)])

val = client.get("my_key")
if val is None:
    val = my_service.some_method(some_args)
    client.set("my_key", val)
do_something(val)
```

This is a pretty typical interaction with a memcache client. Let's assume that HashClient has exactly the same contract for `get` and `set` (with `ignore_exc=False` by default). In that case, if "server1", "server2" and "server3" are all down, we would expect `client.get()` to raise an Exception. If the servers were available when `get` was called, but went down before `set` was called, then we would expect `set` to raise an Exception. If `ignore_exc=True`, then we would expect `get` to never raise (it would just return `None` when the servers were down), but `set` would still raise if the servers were unavailable.

What I'm pointing out is that the actual contract for `HashClient` has it always returning `None` from both `get` and `set` if the hashed server is down (even if some of the other servers are up, as we don't do retries on socket errors). That means `HashClient` acts like `ignore_exc=True` for both `get` and `set` methods at all times (and is not configurable). It's actually a little bit worse than that, as `_run_cmd()` always returns `None` when there is a socket error, and that breaks the contract of functions like `cas` and `get_many`, which either have a specific meaning for `None`, or should not ever return it (respectively). The implementation of `ignore_exc` in `Client` is careful to do the right thing for those cases.

There's no reason to choose between gracefully handling failures and supporting the `Client` contract. It is easy to do both. If you are uncomfortable with `ignore_exc`, then the simple way to do both is to add these lines:

```
...
except socket.error:
    exc_info = sys.exc_inf()
    ... your current failure logic ...
    raise exc_info[0], exc_info[1], exc_info[2]
```

If you would like to support the `ignore_exc` feature in `HashingClient` you'll have to wrap the last line of that in a conditional that checks the memcache call name and the value of ignore_exc and decides whether to return a value (we return `{}` in Client in that case) or raise.

FWIW, the `ignore_exc` feature is widely used (from what other users have told me), and is turned on by default at Pinterest, mostly because we do not want memcache server outages to cause site outages, and we don't want to wrap all calls to `get` in try/except clauses. I think it is worth supporting for `HashClient`, but I don't consider it a blocking change.

I think this is probably well known, but I'm getting myself back up to speed and (re?)realizing the minimal strategy / minimize goal can lead to masked problems when mixed with dependency resolution that is context sensitive. A concrete example follows (~adjacency list notation):
```
a -> b -> c
a -> x2

d -> b -> c
d -> x3

b -> x1
```

Here is a graph with a minimal cover of 2 sets, `{a,b,c,x1,x2}` & `{d,b,c,x1,x3}`.
In this graph, `x1`, `x2`, and `x3` represent 3 versions of a single logical external dependency, say the python `six` library, and an external dependency resolution task will collapse `x1` and `x2` from the 1st set to, say `x2` and similarly `x1`, `x3` from the second set to `x3`. Lets say a downstream task uses these new resolved sets: `{a,b,c,x2}` & `{d,b,c,x3}` and the task succeeds. This can mask the fact that an interior covered graph, namely `{b, x1}`, fails. The issue here being an interior graph (library), is broken on its own and only found working in combination with the current roots that depend on it. Later, add in a new root with no `x` deps, but a dep on `b`, and that new root will fail because `b` on its own fails, which is a surprise since `b` worked under a different minimal cover previously.

This is all to say, I'm not sure if the minimal strategy or minimal cover goal should be supported! In practice they tend to work and speed things up, but the failure mode described here is built-in and perhaps non-obvious. Maybe this is too useful to jettison and just deserves wiki-level docs at some point.

I have no problem with including assumptions (even homebrew-specific ones) in the plist because users understand how to change them, but using the wrong locale may break things in a very subtle way on POSIX (corrupted filenames etc), if theoretically your real locale is a very obscure one. I don't know if such a locale is even possible on OS X. But either way, it is technically wrong to assume any hardcoded value.

I figured there must be a way to get the proper locale variables, maybe as an extra command before vdirsyncer. I think at this point we would need some third-party opinion/knowledge.

2) the message is ambiguous. Imagine when you read `task id is xxx` in tons of log messages. Do you have any idea what this message is about? Thus, as a good practice, we should always be specific about what a log message is about. In this case, it can be something like `Reconstructing objects for task xxx, objects ids are xxx.`.
3) Note that `UniqueIdHelper.computeTaskId` will always be called no matter logging level is below INFO or not. In this case, the computation may not be very heavy. But it's still better to protect the logging call with `if (logger.isInfoEnable())`, to avoid unnecessary computation.
